title: The Person and Work of Christ, Part 10
speaker: Elder Scott Hollander
source_files: 6–16-24 - Person and Work of Chist - Part 10 - Elder Scott Hollander.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Title 'Son of Man' and Its Relevance to the Second Coming
subpoints: Correction from a previous lesson: the title 'Son of Man' was omitted from the discussion of Christ's kingship but belongs there — it originates in Daniel 7 and refers primarily to Christ's dominion and kingdom
Daniel 7:13-14: the Son of Man comes before the Ancient of Days and receives dominion, glory, and a kingdom — an everlasting dominion that shall not pass away
The New Testament references to Christ as 'Son of Man' frequently echo Daniel 7's kingdom and authority language
Revelation 5 also echoes Daniel 7, as the Lamb is given authority — there are questions about whether the Lamb opening the scroll represents His prophetic, priestly, and kingly offices simultaneously

outline.title: WLC Q. 56: How Is Christ to Be Exalted in His Coming Again to Judge the World?
subpoints: Answer: He who was unjustly judged and condemned by wicked men shall come again at the last day in great power, in the full manifestation of His own glory and of His Father's, with all His holy angels, with a shout, with a voice of the archangel, and with a trumpet of God, to judge the world in righteousness
First coming: to bring salvation — John 3:17: 'God did not send His Son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world through Him might be saved'

Second coming: to bring judgment — John 5:25-29: 'An hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear His voice and come out — those who have done good to the resurrection of life, and those who have done evil to the resurrection of judgment'

outline.title: The Irony of Justice: The Unjust Judge Becomes the Just Judge
subpoints: In His first coming, Jesus was fully innocent yet was unjustly judged and condemned by wicked men — 2 Corinthians 5:21: 'He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us'
Acts 3:14-15: 'You denied the Holy One and the Just, and asked for a murderer to be granted to you, and killed the Prince of life'
In His second coming, He will return in great power and vindicate Himself — Matthew 24:29-31: 'They will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory'
Acts 17:31: God has appointed a day on which He will judge the world in righteousness by the Man whom He has ordained; He has given assurance by raising Him from the dead
2 Thessalonians 1:3-10: Christ will return to give rest to believers and to take vengeance on those who do not obey the gospel; He comes to be glorified in His saints

outline.title: The Second Coming in the Seventh Seal, Trumpet, and Bowl of Revelation
subpoints: Revelation 8:5 (seventh seal): fire thrown to earth, with noises, thunderings, lightnings, and an earthquake

Revelation 11:15-19 (seventh trumpet): 'The kingdoms of this world have become the kingdoms of our Lord and of His Christ' — the nations were angry, Your wrath has come; the temple of God opened in heaven
Revelation 16:17-21 (seventh bowl): 'It is done!' — a great earthquake and great hail
Interpretation note: the seven seals, seven trumpets, and seven bowls are concurrent rather than sequential; the seventh of each pictures the preparation and the coming of Christ
Luke 9:26: 'Whoever is ashamed of Me and My words, of him the Son of Man will be ashamed when He comes in His own glory and in His Father's and of the holy angels'
Matthew 25:31: 'When the Son of Man comes in His glory, and all the holy angels with Him, then He will sit on the throne of His glory'

outline.title: Four Things We Know About the Second Coming
subpoints: (1) It will be definite and personal: Acts 1:9-11 — the same Jesus will return in the same manner; it is a specific person, not a concept
(2) It will be visible and public: Revelation 1:7 — 'Every eye will see Him, even those who pierced Him'; there will be no secret rapture known only to the church
(3) It will be miraculous and supernatural: 1 Corinthians 15:22; 1 Thessalonians 4:16 — 'The dead in Christ will rise first' — a supernatural resurrection event

(4) It will be sudden and specific: Matthew 24:27 (like lightning from east to west); 1 Corinthians 15:51-52 (in the twinkling of an eye); Matthew 24:36 — the day and hour are known only to the Father, but there is a specific appointed day

outline.title: The Rapture, Parousia, and the Single Event of Christ's Return
subpoints: The word parousia (1 Thessalonians 4:13-18) means 'coming or arrival' — the dead in Christ will rise first, then the living saints will be caught up together with them to meet the Lord in the air

The word for 'meeting' in 1 Thessalonians 4:17 is used only 3-4 times in the NT and always describes going out to greet a dignitary and usher him into the city — not a permanent departure from the city
The saints meet the Lord in the air to escort Him back to earth, not to depart to heaven with Him — Paul says 'and so we shall always be with the Lord'
The word epiphany (the appearance/manifestation of Christ) and apocalypse (revelation) also describe this single event
Scripture presents the rapture, second coming, and day of the Lord as a singular event in which believers meet the Lord in the air and escort Him back to earth in His triumphant return

outline.title: The Millennium and the Final Judgment
subpoints: Revelation 20 describes a thousand-year reign; the speaker holds to a premillennial view — a literal future kingdom of Christ on earth, though not necessarily exactly 1000 years

During this kingdom: Christ reigns over nations, disputes are settled, death still exists (Isaiah 65: a man who dies at 100 years is considered a child), and births continue among unbelieving remnants
At the end of the millennium, Satan is released, a final rebellion occurs, and then the final defeat of Satan and the judgment of unbelievers

Revelation 20:11-15: the great white throne judgment; the book of life is opened; those not in it are cast into the lake of fire — the second death and the final destruction of death (1 Corinthians 15:26)
For believers, we will not be condemned (Romans 8); we will stand in the judgment; we are in Christ

summary: This is Part 10 and the final lesson in the Person and Work of Christ Sunday school series, covering Westminster Larger Catechism Question 56 on Christ's exaltation in His return to judge the world. Elder Scott Hollander begins by returning to an earlier omission — the title 'Son of Man' and its origin in Daniel 7's dominion and kingdom language — before working through Q. 56. He notes the irony of justice at the heart of the second coming: the One who was unjustly condemned in His first coming will return as the righteous judge. He explores the descriptions of Christ's return in the seventh seal, trumpet, and bowl of Revelation (which he reads as concurrent, not sequential), and summarizes four things Scripture tells us about the second coming: it will be definite and personal, visible and public (no secret rapture), miraculous and supernatural, and sudden yet on a specific appointed day. He then engages the Greek words parousia, epiphany, and apokalypsis, and argues from the meaning of the word 'meeting' in 1 Thessalonians 4:17 that the saints who are caught up in the air are escorting Christ back to earth, not departing to heaven. He concludes with a premillennial view of Revelation 20, arguing for a literal future earthly kingdom of Christ (though not necessarily exactly 1,000 years), the continuation of natural life and death within that kingdom among unbelievers, and finally the great white throne judgment of the wicked and the ultimate defeat of death.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Son of Man and His Kingdom

We are continuing our series through the Larger Catechism on the person and work of Christ. Today we will be looking at Question 56. Before we jump into it, I wanted to address something from when I spoke a few weeks ago.

When I was discussing the titles of the kingship of Jesus, I did not have the Son of Man on my list. That was an oversight. The New Testament references to the Son of Man go back to Daniel 7, where this title originates, and that is precisely what Daniel 7 talks about — the kingship of the Son of Man. And as Christ returns in His exaltation and power and glory, there are references throughout the New Testament to Him returning as the Son of Man, wielding authority and power to judge.

Daniel 7:13-14: "I was watching in the night visions, and behold, one like the Son of Man, coming with the clouds of heaven! He came to the Ancient of Days, and they brought Him near before Him. Then to Him was given dominion and glory and a kingdom, that all peoples, nations, and languages should serve Him. His dominion is an everlasting dominion, which shall not pass away, and His kingdom the one which shall not be destroyed."

So there is whole lot of dominion and kingdom language tied to the Son of Man. There are also echoes between Daniel 7 and Revelation 5, where the Lamb receives authority — there are good questions about whether Christ's ability to open the scroll is related to His threefold office of prophet, priest, and king all at once.

## WLC Q. 56: How Is Christ Exalted in His Coming to Judge the World?

Question 56: How is Christ to be exalted in His coming again to judge the world? The answer: Christ is to be exalted in His coming again to judge the world in that He who was unjustly judged and condemned by wicked men shall come again at the last day in great power, and in the full manifestation of His own glory and of His Father's, with all His holy angels, with a shout, with a voice of the archangel, and with a trumpet of God, to judge the world in righteousness.

What we first want to consider is that in Jesus' first coming, His purpose was to bring salvation. John 3:17: "For God did not send His Son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world through Him might be saved." But in John 5:25-29: "Truly, truly, I say to you, an hour is coming, and is now here, when the dead will hear the voice of the Son of God, and those who hear will live... Do not marvel at this, for an hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear His voice and come out — those who have done good to the resurrection of life, and those who have done evil to the resurrection of judgment." So His first coming was to bring salvation; His second coming is to bring judgment.

## The Irony of Justice

We observe the irony of justice. In Jesus' first coming, He was unjustly judged and condemned by wicked men. 2 Corinthians 5:21: "For He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in Him." The perfect God-man came to earth, was unjustly judged, and took the weight of our sin. Acts 3:14-15: "But you denied the Holy One and the Just, and asked for a murderer to be granted to you, and killed the Prince of life, whom God raised from the dead."

But Jesus is also vindicated by great power. Matthew 24:29-31: "Immediately after the tribulation of those days the sun will be darkened, and the moon will not give its light; the stars will fall from heaven, and the powers of the heavens will be shaken. Then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in heaven, and then all the tribes of the earth will mourn, and they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory. And He will send His angels with a great sound of a trumpet, and they will gather together His elect from the four winds." The vindication is of His own unjust suffering, and it is also the vindication of His elect.

Acts 17:31: "He has appointed a day on which He will judge the world in righteousness by the Man whom He has ordained. He has given assurance of this to all by raising Him from the dead."

2 Thessalonians 1:3-10: "It is a righteous thing with God to repay with tribulation those who trouble you, and to give you who are troubled rest with us when the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven with His mighty angels, in flaming fire taking vengeance on those who do not know God, on those who do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ... When He comes, in that day, to be glorified in His saints and to be admired among all those who believe."

So the second coming vindicates both Christ and His suffering saints.

## The Second Coming in the Book of Revelation

Revelation describes Christ's coming in powerful images. The seventh seal in Revelation 8:5: the angel fills the censer with fire from the altar and throws it to the earth — there were noises, thunderings, lightnings, and an earthquake. This description of noises, thunderings, lightnings, and earthquake is repeated throughout Scripture when speaking of that future day.

Revelation 11:15-19 (seventh trumpet): "The seventh angel sounded, and there were loud voices in heaven, saying, 'The kingdoms of this world have become the kingdoms of our Lord and of His Christ, and He shall reign forever and ever!'" The 24 elders fall on their faces and worship God, saying: "We give You thanks, O Lord God Almighty, the One who is and who was and who is to come, because You have taken Your great power and reigned. The nations were angry, and Your wrath has come, and the time of the dead, that they should be judged, and that You should reward Your servants the prophets and the saints." Then the temple of God was opened in heaven — noises, thunderings, lightnings, and an earthquake and great hail.

The seventh bowl in Revelation 16: the angel pours it into the air and a loud voice from the throne says, "It is done!" — and a great earthquake, the likes of which had never been seen — and great hail.

I interpret the seven seals, trumpets, and bowls as concurrent, not sequential — that the seventh of each gives a picture of the preparation for and the coming back of Christ to earth.

Luke 9:26: "Whoever is ashamed of Me and My words, of him the Son of Man will be ashamed when He comes in His own glory, and in His Father's, and of the holy angels." Matthew 25:31: "When the Son of Man comes in His glory, and all the holy angels with Him, then He will sit on the throne of His glory."

## Four Things We Know About the Second Coming

First, it will be definite and personal. Acts 1:9-11: "This same Jesus, who was taken up from you into heaven, will so come in like manner as you saw Him go into heaven." It is definite because the angels declared He would return. It is personal because it is the same Jesus these disciples knew.

Second, it will be visible and public. Revelation 1:7: "Behold, He is coming with clouds, and every eye will see Him, even they who pierced Him. And all the tribes of the earth will mourn because of Him." There will be no secret rapture visible or experienced only by the church — every eye will see Him.

Third, it will be miraculous and supernatural. 1 Corinthians 15:22: "As in Adam all die, even so in Christ all shall be made alive." 1 Thessalonians 4:16: "The Lord Himself will descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of an archangel, and with the trumpet of God. And the dead in Christ will rise first." The dead believer in Christ will be raised — miraculous and supernatural.

Fourth, it will be sudden and specific. Matthew 24:27: "As the lightning comes from the east and flashes to the west, so also will the coming of the Son of Man be" — sudden. 1 Corinthians 15:51-52: "In a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet." Matthew 24:36: "But of that day and hour no one knows, not even the angels of heaven, but My Father only." There is a specific day on heaven's calendar. Jesus declared it known only to the Father — but it is a specific day, and it will come suddenly.

## The Parousia, Meeting, and Return to Earth

Scripture presents the rapture, second coming, and day of the Lord as a singular event. Paul uses the word parousia in 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18 — meaning "coming or arrival": "For since we believe that Jesus died and rose again, even so, through Jesus, God will bring with him those who have fallen asleep... For the Lord Himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God. And the dead in Christ will rise first. Then we who are alive, who are left, will be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air, and so we will always be with the Lord."

The key word is "meeting" — in Greek, this word is used only three or four times in the New Testament, and every time it describes going out to greet a dignitary outside the city and to escort him back into the city. It does not describe departing from the city and never returning. So when the saints are caught up in the air to meet the Lord, they are going out to greet Him and escort Him back to earth — not departing from earth permanently.

Paul also says in verse 14 that God will bring with Jesus those who have fallen asleep. So the dead believers who are already in heaven come with Jesus in His return. Then the living saints are caught up to meet Him in the air, and together they escort Jesus back to earth.

This reconciles Paul saying the saints are coming "with" Christ and the saints "meeting" the Lord — it is all one event. No one in Scripture states that the saints meet the Lord in the air and then return with Him to heaven. We will meet the Lord in the air, we will return with Him to earth, and we will be with Him forever.

## The Millennium and the Final Judgment

Revelation 20 describes a thousand-year reign. I hold to a premillennial view — I believe there is a literal future physical reign of Christ on earth, though not necessarily exactly 1,000 years. George Eldon Ladd writes that while we need not take "a thousand years" literally, it does appear to represent a real period of time, however long or short that may be.

Isaiah 65 speaks of a man who dies at a hundred years old being considered a child — implying that death still exists in this future kingdom. There are references to nations and disputes being settled, and to births continuing among those who are not yet believers. So during this kingdom, natural human life continues alongside believers who reign with Christ.

At the end of this period, Satan is released, there is a final rebellion, Christ defeats it, and then comes the great white throne judgment. Revelation 20:11-15: the books are opened, the book of life is opened, and those not found in the book of life are cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death. And here we see the fulfillment of 1 Corinthians 15:26: the last enemy that will be destroyed is death.

For believers, we will not be condemned in that judgment. We are in Christ. Romans 8 tells us there is no condemnation. We will stand, because we are right with God — not by our own righteousness but by the righteousness of Christ.

Our Heavenly Father, we rejoice that Your Scripture demonstrates and the Westminster standards illustrate for us the teaching of Scripture, that You will come in great power and glory. You were unjustly treated in Your first coming. We know that You are the king upon the throne and You will come in glory and power to vindicate those who suffer for Your namesake. We rejoice in that day; we want to have hope in it. We ask that You would come quickly, Lord Jesus, and keep us faithful until then. In Christ's name. Amen.
